high_value_elements: Identifies uncontrolled postoperative pain as the primary active problem
Recognizes need to distinguish expected pain from a surgical complication
Notes panting and tachycardia as pain indicators, not just incidental findings
Identifies inadequacy of the current buprenorphine-based protocol as a contributing factor
Acknowledges contralateral limb guarding as a potential secondary concern
major_concerns: Fails to recognize severity of pain or treats vocalizing/panting as expected without assessment
Misses the possibility of a surgical complication entirely
Focuses on minor findings (e.g., reduced appetite) before addressing pain or stability
Identifies pain but makes no attempt to assess analgesic protocol adequacy

high_value_elements: Places pain control and patient comfort as the immediate first priority
Sequences reassessment of surgical site before ordering new diagnostics
Defers lower-urgency concerns until patient is stabilized and comfortable
Appropriately integrates monitoring and reassessment into the priority sequence
major_concerns: Delays pain intervention to pursue low-yield diagnostics first
Orders extensive workup before any analgesic escalation
Treats all findings as equally urgent without clinical ranking
Jumps to intervention without any assessment rationale

high_value_elements: Considers expected postoperative pain versus implant failure, compartment syndrome, or infection
Proposes focused reassessment (limb exam, wound palpation) before advanced diagnostics
Requests additional radiographs or ultrasound only with clear clinical rationale
Does not reflexively request a full diagnostic panel when targeted assessment is more appropriate
Interprets available normal CBC/chem/radiographs meaningfully in the clinical context
major_concerns: Pursues shotgun diagnostics without articulating what each would reveal
Fails to consider any plausible cause of pain escalation beyond 'expected soreness'
Ignores that post-op radiographs already show implant integrity
Confuses diagnostic ordering with clinical decision-making

high_value_elements: Transitions to a full mu-opioid agonist (e.g., methadone, hydromorphone) given buprenorphine ceiling effect
Employs multimodal analgesia: opioid + NSAID (if no contraindication) + adjunct such as gabapentin, ketamine CRI, or lidocaine CRI
Continues or appropriately adjusts NSAID with attention to gastric and renal safety
Includes a structured reassessment plan with defined frequency and pain score targets
Addresses nursing care, environmental comfort, and owner communication
major_concerns: Proposes only a single analgesic agent for a patient in obvious uncontrolled pain
Does not escalate opioid despite clear inadequacy of current protocol
Adds analgesics without considering interactions or cumulative risks
Provides no monitoring plan or follow-up checkpoints
Switches NSAID without rationale or adds second NSAID (contraindicated)

high_value_elements: Recognizes buprenorphine ceiling effect as a factor limiting current protocol
Addresses risks of opioid side effects (sedation, dysphoria, GI effects) with monitoring plan
Notes NSAID considerations in an older large-breed dog (renal function, hydration status)
Acknowledges uncertainty if clinical picture worsens despite escalation
Defines clear escalation triggers and contingency actions
Considers patient fall risk and physical therapy implications post-escalation
major_concerns: No attention to medication risks or side effects
Does not mention any monitoring parameters
Fails to recognize that buprenorphine is a partial agonist with limited ceiling
No contingency plan if initial escalation fails
Ignores NSAID safety considerations in this patient profile

high_value_elements: Explains the pharmacological rationale for opioid selection (full vs. partial agonist, receptor kinetics)
Provides guideline-consistent reasoning for multimodal approach
Articulates tradeoffs between alternative strategies without dismissing them
Demonstrates understanding of TPLO-specific pain pathways and expected recovery trajectory
Cites relevant considerations at the specialist level, not just general practitioner defaults
major_concerns: Provides only conclusory statements without pharmacological or clinical justification
Cannot explain why one opioid is preferred over another in this context
Reasoning is at generalist level, not specialist level
Cannot defend choices when challenged
Dismisses alternatives without engaging with their merits
